# Glossary

The following terms appear in the main text and may be unfamiliar to some 
readers. We define them in the context of this tutorial rather than in full 
generality.

**Containerization.** Packaging an application together with its dependencies
into a single isolated unit, a *container*, so that it runs the same way on
different machines. Docker is the most common tool. Containerization bundles
software, but it does not by itself guarantee that the bundled versions can be
rebuilt identically in the future, which is the gap Nix addresses.

**Functional programming language.** A type of programming language built around
functions whose output depends only on their inputs, without hidden changes to
shared state. Nix is such a language, and this property is what makes its builds
reproducible. For this tutorial the only practical consequence is that Nix's
language is unfamiliar to most R users, which is precisely why {rix} writes it
for you. Thus, no knowledge of functional programming is required to follow 
along.

**Mutable upstream repositories.** The online sources from which software is
downloaded (its *repositories*) can change over time, that is, they are
*mutable*. When a build refers to "the latest version" from such a source,
rebuilding it later may pull different software, so the result drifts. Pinning a
fixed version, as Nix does, avoids this.

**R interpreter.** The program that reads R code and executes it. Installing "R"
means installing this interpreter. Two computers can run the same R *version*
yet still differ in how the interpreter itself was built (for example, the
compiler used or the system libraries it was linked against), and such
differences can change numerical results. Pinning the environment fixes the
interpreter, not just the version number.

**System-level libraries.** Shared software components installed on the
operating system, outside of R, that many R packages rely on to function, such
as libraries for compression, linear algebra (e.g., BLAS and LAPACK), or the C++
backends used by some packages. They are not installed by `install.packages()`
and must already be present on the system. Differences in these libraries can
make code fail or produce different results across machines.

**Workflow orchestration.** Defining and automating the order in which the steps
of an analysis run (for example, generate data, then fit models, then compute
performance metrics, then make plots), tracking which steps depend on which, and
re-running only the steps affected by a change. The {targets} package is a common
example.
